From Intuition to a Numerical Example
This article is inspired by the simulation-based conformal prediction framework discussed in Conformal Prediction for Simulation Models by Benjamin LeRoy and Chad Schafer.
Article Roadmap
We will build conformal prediction step by step:

• Motivation for Conformal Prediction

• Why point predictions are not enough?

• What conformal prediction promises?

• The role of calibration and exchangeability

• Split conformal prediction

• Simulation-based conformal prediction

• Nested sets, pseudo-density, and neighborhoods

• A complete numerical example by hand

• Key takeaways
1. Motivation for Conformal Prediction
Point predictions are everywhere in machine learning and statistics. A model may predict tomorrow’s temperature, a patient’s risk score, the price of a house, or the expected output of a simulator. These predictions are useful, but they are incomplete.
A point prediction gives us a single number. It does not tell us how uncertain the model is.
For example, suppose a model predicts that the response will be 10. Should we expect the true value to be between 9 and 11? Between 5 and 15? Or could there be two separate plausible regions, such as values near 7 and values near 13?
This is where conformal prediction becomes useful.
Conformal prediction provides a way to construct prediction intervals, or prediction sets with a user-specified coverage level. Instead of only asking for a single predicted value, we ask for a set that is likely to contain the future response.
Informally, if we target 90% coverage, we want our prediction set to contain the true future response about 90% of the time.
The attractive feature of conformal prediction is that this coverage guarantee does not require strong distributional assumptions such as normality. Under the key assumption of exchangeability, conformal prediction can provide finite-sample coverage guarantees.
In this article, we will build the idea step by step. We will start from the intuition behind conformal prediction, move to calibration and prediction sets, and then work through a small numerical example by hand. The example will include simulations, pseudo-density scores, neighborhoods, nested sets, ranks, conformity scores, and the final calibrated prediction set.
2. Why Point Predictions Are Not Enough
Most machine learning models are designed to produce point predictions.
A regression model may predict that a house will sell for $400,000. A weather model may predict that tomorrow’s temperature will be 75°F. A simulation model may predict one likely value of a future response.
Point predictions are useful because they are simple and easy to interpret. However, they hide uncertainty.
Suppose a model predicts:
Predicted response = 10
This number alone does not tell us whether the true response is likely to be close to 10 or whether there is substantial uncertainty around it.
For example, all of the following situations could produce the same point prediction:

• The true response is usually between 9.8 and 10.2.

• The true response is usually between 7 and 13.

• The true response is sometimes near 7 and sometimes near 13, with very little probability near 10.
In the first case, the model is highly certain. In the second case, the model is uncertain but roughly centered around 10. In the third case, the point prediction may even fall in a region that is not very plausible.
This is why we often need more than a point prediction. We need a prediction interval or prediction set that describes a range of plausible future responses.
Conformal prediction gives us a principled way to construct such sets.
3. What Conformal Prediction Promises
Conformal prediction is a framework for turning model predictions into prediction sets with a desired coverage level.
Instead of only predicting one value, we may want a prediction set for a new input, written as C(X_new). The goal is for the future response, Y_new, to fall inside this set with high probability.
For example, if we want 90% coverage, then the prediction set should contain the true future response about 90% of the time.
More generally, we choose a miscoverage level, written as alpha. The target coverage level is then 1 − alpha.
This does not mean every single prediction set will contain the true response. Some future responses will still fall outside the set. The guarantee is about long-run coverage over repeated future observations.
For example, if we use 90% conformal prediction sets for many future observations, then approximately 90% of those true responses should be covered, assuming the required conditions hold.
The most important condition is exchangeability. Informally, the calibration data and the future test point should come from the same data-generating process. When this condition holds, conformal prediction can provide a finite-sample coverage guarantee without requiring the response distribution to be normal.
4. Calibration and Exchangeability
The central idea in conformal prediction is calibration.
We first use a calibration dataset to measure how unusual past observations look under our model or scoring rule. Then we use those calibration scores to decide how wide, large, or flexible the future prediction set should be.
This article is inspired by the simulation-based conformal prediction framework discussed in Conformal Prediction for Simulation Models by Benjamin LeRoy and Chad Schafer. The paper builds prediction regions by using simulated outcomes, scoring how central or unusual a candidate response is, and then calibrating those scores using observed data.
The key assumption behind the conformal guarantee is exchangeability.
Informally, exchangeability means that the calibration observations and the future test observation should be statistically comparable. They should come from the same data-generating process, or at least be interchangeable in distribution.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is important because conformal prediction uses the calibration scores as a reference for what future scores should look like. If the future observation behaves like the calibration observations, then its score should look like one more draw from the same score distribution.
That is what allows conformal prediction to choose a threshold from calibration data and use it for future prediction.
In simple terms:
The calibration set teaches us what “normal error” or “normal unusualness” looks like.
The future point is then judged against that same standard.
If the future point is generated in the same way as the calibration data, the conformal ranking argument works. If not, the guarantee may no longer apply.
5. Split Conformal Prediction
The simplest way to understand conformal prediction is through split conformal prediction.
In split conformal prediction, the available data are divided into separate parts. One part is used to train the predictive model, and another part is used for calibration.
The training set is used in the usual machine learning sense. We fit a model that predicts the response from the input.
The calibration set is different. It is not used to fit the model. Instead, it is used to measure how large the model’s errors tend to be on fresh data.
For example, suppose our model gives a prediction for each calibration input. We compare each prediction with the actual calibration response and calculate a calibration residual.
Large residuals mean the model made a larger error. Small residuals mean the model was more accurate.
After computing all calibration residuals, we choose a high quantile of those residuals. This value becomes the conformal threshold.
For a new input, the model gives a point prediction. We then expand that point prediction by the calibrated threshold to form a prediction interval.
In this way, the model provides the center of the interval, while the calibration set provides the width of the interval.
The important point is that calibration is not the same as training. Training learns the prediction rule. Calibration learns how uncertain that prediction rule tends to be.
6. Simulation-Based Conformal Prediction
Split conformal prediction is easy to understand when we have a fitted model and residuals. However, some problems are more naturally described through simulations.
In many scientific and statistical settings, we may not have a simple formula for the conditional distribution of the response. But we may be able to simulate possible responses for a given input.
That is the setting considered in Conformal Prediction for Simulation Models by Benjamin LeRoy and Chad Schafer.
Instead of relying only on a point prediction, the method uses simulated outcomes from the conditional response distribution. For a fixed input, we generate many possible responses. These simulations show what kinds of outcomes are plausible for that input.
The main question then becomes:
How do we turn these simulated outcomes into a prediction region with conformal coverage?
The paper’s approach is to rank simulated outcomes by how central they are, build nested prediction sets from those simulations, and then use calibration data to choose the final set.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The simulations determine the shape of the prediction region.
The calibration data determine how far into the nested sequence we should go.
This is especially useful when the response distribution is not simple. For example, the response may be skewed, multimodal, or irregular. In such cases, a standard interval around a point prediction may not describe the true uncertainty very well.
Simulation-based conformal prediction allows the prediction region to follow the shape suggested by the simulated outcomes.
7. Nested Sets, Pseudo-Density, and Neighborhoods
Now we need a way to convert simulated outcomes into a prediction region.
Suppose we fix an input value and generate several simulated responses. Some simulated responses will be located in dense regions, surrounded by many other simulations. Others may be isolated or far from the main group.
The basic idea is to treat dense simulated outcomes as more central and isolated simulated outcomes as less central.
To measure centrality, the paper uses a pseudo-density score. For each simulated outcome, we compare it with all other simulated outcomes. If it is close to many others, it receives a high pseudo-density score. If it is far from most others, it receives a low pseudo-density score.
Once the simulations are scored, we rank them from most central to least central.
Next, we place a small neighborhood around each simulated outcome. In one dimension, this neighborhood is simply an interval around the simulated value.
Then we construct nested sets.
The largest simulation-based set includes neighborhoods around all simulated outcomes. The next set removes the least central simulation. The next one removes another low-density simulation, and so on. As we move deeper into the sequence, the sets become smaller and more central.
A candidate response receives a conformity score based on the deepest nested set that still contains it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If the candidate survives into a very small, central set, it receives a high conformity score. If it only appears in a large, loose set, it receives a low conformity score. If it is outside all simulation neighborhoods, it receives a score of zero.
This gives us a complete scoring rule. Calibration can then choose the score threshold needed to form the final conformal prediction region.
8. A Complete Numerical Example by Hand
We now work through a small numerical example.
The goal is not to create a realistic large-scale conformal prediction system. Instead, the goal is to make every step visible: simulations, distances, pseudo-density scores, ranks, neighborhoods, nested sets, calibration scores, and the final prediction set.
